zh.lifeSummary: 當Lady Eleanor Holland, Countess March, countess salisbury, baroness chereton於1370年10月13日出生在英國藍卡爾武普 霍蘭時，她的父親Thomas de Holland 2nd Earl of Kent20歲，母親Alice FitzAlan21歲。她於1388年10月7日與lord sir Roger Mortimer 4th Earl Of March結婚。他們至少是2個兒子和3個女兒的父母。她去世於1405年10月18日在英國英格蘭肯特貝嫩登享年35歲，埋葬在英國英格蘭波克夏比善姆。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Lady Eleanor Holland, Countess March, countess salisbury, baroness chereton于1370年10月13日出生在英国藍卡爾武普 霍蘭时，她的父亲Thomas de Holland 2nd Earl of Kent20岁，母亲Alice FitzAlan21岁。她于1388年10月7日与lord sir Roger Mortimer 4th Earl Of March结婚。他们至少是2个儿子和3个女儿的父母。她去世于1405年10月18日在英国英格兰肯特貝嫩登享年35岁，埋葬在英国英格兰波克夏比善姆。

nameMeanings.name: Holland,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English, German, Dutch, Danish, Norwegian, Swedish, French, and Jewish (Ashkenazic): habitational name from Holland, a province of the Netherlands.

English: habitational name from Downholland or Upholland (Lancashire), Hulland (Derbyshire), the Parts of Holland, one of the three administrative subdivisions of Lincolnshire, any of the four places called Hoyland (southern Yorkshire), and possibly Great and Little Holland (Essex). The placenames all derive from Old English hōh ‘heel, spur of land’ + land ‘land’.

English: habitational name either from Hoeland (Farm) in Bury (Sussex), or from Holland's Barn in Albourne (Sussex). The placename in Bury has the same etymology as in 1 above, while the placename in Albourne may derive from Old English hol ‘hole, hollow’ + land ‘land’.

variants.name: Hollands
meanings: English: variant of Holland , especially 3, with post-medieval excrescent -s.
Dutch: genitivized form of   Holland  .

variants.name: Howland
meanings: English: variant of   Holland  .
Americanized form of Norwegian   Hovland  .

History: Howland was the name of three Quaker brothers, original settlers in Marshfield, MA. They were from Huntingdonshire, England. The eldest, John Howland (c. 1593–1672) was a passenger on the Mayflower, servant to Gov. John Carver, who died in the first winter at Plymouth Colony.

variants.name: Hollander
meanings: Dutch (also Den Hollander), German (also Holländer), English, Swedish, and Jewish (Ashkenazic): habitational name for someone from Holland (see Holland 1).

variants.name: Holand
meanings: English and Scottish: variant of   Holland  .
Norwegian: habitational name from about twenty farmsteads so named, composed of Old Norse hár ‘high’ + land ‘land, field’, denoting a high-lying farm.

variants.name: Aland
meanings: Scandinavian (Åland): topographic name from the Åland Islands in the Gulf of Bothnia.

English: topographic name from Middle English atte lande (Old French laund) ‘dweller at the glade or pasture’, or sometimes perhaps, ‘dweller at the old cultivated land(s)’ from Old English eald, ald ‘old’ + land ‘land’.

variants.name: Hollon
meanings: English: altered form of   Holland  .

nameMeanings.name: Countess
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Nottinghamshire, of Norman origin): from Middle English contas(e), Old French contesse ‘countess’, applied as a nickname for a proud, haughty woman or for a foppish man, or as an occupational name for a servant of a countess.

variants.name: Countiss
meanings: English: variant of   Countess  .

nameMeanings.name: March,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: habitational name from March (Cambridgeshire) or from any of numerous minor English placenames deriving from Middle English marche ‘boundary’ (partly from Old English mearc in the dative-case form mearce, partly from Old French marche). Compare Mark 2.
Catalan: from the personal name March, Catalan equivalent of Mark 1. This surname is also found in southern France.
German: topographic name from Middle Low German march, Middle High German marc(h) ‘mark, sign, boundary’, or a habitational name from a place called with this word (Bavaria).

variants.name: Marche
meanings: English: variant of   March  .

variants.name: Mark
meanings: English, Dutch, and Sorbian: from Latin Marcus, the personal name of Saint Mark the Evangelist, author of the second Gospel in the New Testament. This name was also borne by a number of early Christian saints. Marcus is an ancient Roman name, of uncertain (possibly non-Italic) etymology; it may have some connection with the name of the war god Mars; compare Martin . The personal name was not as popular in England in the Middle Ages as it was elsewhere in Europe, especially in Italy, where the evangelist became the patron of Venice and the Venetian Republic. He was allegedly buried at Aquileia. In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed many cognates and similar-sounding names from other languages, e.g. Polish, Czech, and Slovak Marek , Ukrainian, Slovenian, Sorbian, Rusyn, and Croatian Marko , Greek Markos , and also their patronymics and other derivatives, e.g. Jewish and Slavic Markowicz and Markovich ; see also 6 below.

English and German; Dutch (Van der Mark): topographic name for someone who lived on a boundary between two districts, from Middle English merke, Middle High German marc, Middle Dutch marke, merke, all meaning ‘borderland’. The German term also denotes an area of fenced-off land (see Marker 5) and, like the English word, is embodied in various placenames which have given rise to habitational names. This surname (in any of the possible senses; see also 5 below) is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine; compare Marck ).
English (of Norman origin): habitational name from Marck, Pas-de-Calais, France.

variants.name: Marzec
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Tadeusz, Boleslaw, Cecylia, Edyta, Halina, Marcin, Stanislaw, Wladyslaw, Zdzislaw, Zigmund, Zosia.

Polish: from marzec ‘March’, a nickname or a personal name for someone who was born or baptized in the month of March or who had some other special connection with the month, such as owing a feudal obligation then. Compare March 4.

variants.name: Marquis
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Emile, Normand, Marcel, Fernand, Lucien, Rosaire, Andre, Armand, Camille, Cecile, Jacques, Francois.

French: from the title marquis ‘marquess’, applied as an ironic nickname to someone who behaved like a marquis or as a metonymic occupational name for a servant in the household of a marquis. The title, derived from an ancient Germanic word meaning ‘boundary’ (compare March ), originally referred to the governor of a border territory. Marquises did not form part of the original French feudal structure of nobility; the title was first adopted by the Counts of Toulouse because of their possessions in the border region beyond the Rhône. Compare Markee and Markey .

English: nickname from Middle English markis ‘marquis, marquess, nobleman’ (Old French marchis); see also 1 above. The word is also found as a female personal name which may also be origin of the surname.

nameMeanings.name: countess
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Nottinghamshire, of Norman origin): from Middle English contas(e), Old French contesse ‘countess’, applied as a nickname for a proud, haughty woman or for a foppish man, or as an occupational name for a servant of a countess.

variants.name: Countiss
meanings: English: variant of   Countess  .

nameMeanings.name: salisbury,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Lancashire): habitational name primarily from Salesbury in Blackburn (Lancashire) but also occasionally from Salisbury (Wiltshire). The Lancashire placename derives from Old English salh ‘willow, sallow’ + burg ‘fortress’, while the Wiltshire placename arises from a shortened form of the Celtic placename Sorviodunum (from an unknown initial element + Celtic dūno- ‘fort’). In the Old English period the second element was dropped and Sorvio- (of unexplained etymology) became Searo- in Old English as the result of folk etymological association the Old English word searu ‘trick’; to this an explanatory burh ‘fortress, manor, town’ was added. The city is recorded in the Domesday Book as Sarisberie; the change of -r- to -l- is the result of later dissimilation.

variants.name: Saulsbury
meanings: English: variant of   Salisbury  .

variants.name: Saulsberry
meanings: Altered form of English   Salisbury  .

variants.name: Salsbery
meanings: English: variant of   Salisbury  .

variants.name: Salsbury
meanings: English (Worcestershire): variant of   Salisbury  .
Jewish (American): adoption of the English surname (see 1 above) in place of Salomon or some other Jewish name beginning with Sa-.

variants.name: Salsberry
meanings: English: variant of   Salisbury  .

nameMeanings.name: baroness
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Norwegian: old variant of Bartnes (and, in North America, probably also an altered form of this), a habitational name from Trøndelag, a form of Old Norse burkn ‘fern’ + nes ‘promontary, headland’.
In some cases possibly also an altered form of German   Bartmess  .

variants.name: Bartmess
meanings: American shortened form of German   Barthelmess  .

nameMeanings.name: chereton
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: English: locative name from Cheriton Fitzpaine, Cheriton Bishop, or Cheriton in Bredon (all Devon).  Compare  Cherrington  .

variants.name: Cherrington
meanings: English: habitational name from any of various places called Cherington or Cherrington. Cherrington in Shropshire is probably named from the Old English personal name Ceorra + -ing- denoting association (or alternatively from Old English cerring ‘river bend’) + tūn ‘settlement, estate’, but others (Cherington in Gloucestershire and Cherrington in Warwickshire) are from Old English cyrice ‘church’ + tūn. Places called Cheriton in Devon, Hampshire, Kent, and Somerset also have this last etymology.

nameMeanings.name: Eleanor
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From an Old French respelling of the Old Provençal name Alienor. This has sometimes been taken as a derivative of Helen , but it is more probably of Germanic derivation (the first element being ali ‘other, foreign’ the second is obscure). The name was introduced to England by Eleanor of Aquitaine ( 1122–1204 ), who came from Aquitaine in south-west France to be the wife of King Henry II . It was also borne by Eleanor of Provence, the wife of Henry III , and Eleanor of Castile, wife of Edward I .

variants.name: Ellenor
meanings: Variant spelling of   Eleanor  , the result of blending with   Ellen .

variants.name: Elenor
meanings: From an Old French respelling of the Old Provençal name Alienor. This has sometimes been taken as a derivative of Helen , but it is more probably of Germanic derivation (the first element being ali ‘other, foreign’ the second is obscure). The name was introduced to England by Eleanor of Aquitaine ( 1122–1204 ), who came from Aquitaine in south-west France to be the wife of King Henry II . It was also borne by Eleanor of Provence, the wife of Henry III , and Eleanor of Castile, wife of Edward I .

variants.name: Elinor
meanings: Variant spelling of   Eleanor .

historicEvents.title: Wycliffe's Bible
historicEvents.description: John Wycliffe, English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, completed a translation of the Bible from the Latin Vulgate into Middle English. This translation is known as Wycliffe's Bible and was completed around 1382.

en.lifeSummary: When Lady Eleanor Holland, Countess March, countess salisbury, baroness chereton was born on 13 October 1370, in Up Holland, Lancashire, England, her father, Thomas de Holland 2nd Earl of Kent, was 20 and her mother, Alice FitzAlan, was 21. She married lord sir Roger Mortimer 4th Earl Of March on 7 October 1388. They were the parents of at least 2 sons and 3 daughters. She died on 18 October 1405, in Benenden, Kent, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 35, and was buried in Bisham, Berkshire, England, United Kingdom.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Lady Eleanor Holland, Countess March, countess salisbury, baroness chereton est née le 13 octobre 1370, à Up Holland, Lancashire, Angleterre, son père, Thomas de Holland 2nd Earl of Kent, avait 20 ans et sa mère, Alice FitzAlan, avait 21 ans. Elle a épousé lord sir Roger Mortimer 4th Earl Of March le 7 octobre 1388. Ils ont eu au moins 2 fils et 3 filles. Elle est décédée le 18 octobre 1405, à Benenden, Kent, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 35 ans, et a été enterrée à Bisham, Berkshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni.

de.lifeSummary: Als Lady Eleanor Holland, Countess March, countess salisbury, baroness chereton am 13. Oktober 1370 in Up Holland, Lancashire, England geboren wurde, war ihr Vater Thomas de Holland 2nd Earl of Kent 20 und ihre Mutter Alice FitzAlan 21. Sie hat am 7. Oktober 1388 lord sir Roger Mortimer 4th Earl Of March geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 2 Söhne und 3 Töchter. Sie starb am 18. Oktober 1405 im Alter von 35 Jahren in Benenden, Kent, England, United Kingdom und wurde in Bisham, Berkshire, England, United Kingdom begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Lady Eleanor Holland, Countess March, countess salisbury, baroness chereton è nata il il 13 ottobre 1370 nel Up Holland, Lancashire, England, sua padre, Thomas de Holland 2nd Earl of Kent, aveva 20 anni e sua madre, Alice FitzAlan, aveva 21 anni. Lei sposato lord sir Roger Mortimer 4th Earl Of March il 7 ottobre 1388. Erano genitori di almeno 2 figli e 3 figlie. Morì il 18 ottobre 1405, nel Benenden, Kent, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, all'età di 35 anni, e fu sepolta a Bisham, Berkshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito.

ja.lifeSummary: Lady Eleanor Holland, Countess March, countess salisbury, baroness cheretonが1370年10月13日にイギリスランカシュアイアウップ ホーランドで生まれた時，父Thomas de Holland 2nd Earl of Kentは20歳，母Alice FitzAlanは21歳であった。1388年10月7日にlord sir Roger Mortimer 4th Earl Of Marchと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも2人の息子と3人の娘の親であった。彼女は1405年10月18日にイギリスイギリスケントベンエンデンで35歳で死去し，イギリスイギリスバークシュアイアビシャムにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Lady Eleanor Holland, Countess March, countess salisbury, baroness chereton(이)가 1370년 10월 13일 영국 랭카실르 업 홀랜드에서 태어났을 때, 그녀의 아버지 Thomas de Holland 2nd Earl of Kent(은)는 20세였고, 그녀의 어머니 Alice FitzAlan(은)는 21세였다. 그녀는 1388년 10월 7일 lord sir Roger Mortimer 4th Earl Of March(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 두 명의 아들과 세 명의 딸을 두었다. 그녀는 1405년 10월 18일 영국 잉글랜드 켄트 베니든에서 35세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 잉글랜드 버크실르 비샤에 묻혔다.

pt.13 October 1370–18 October 1405: 13 de outubro de 1370–18 de outubro de 1405
pt.Up Holland,Wigan,Lancashire,England: Up Holland, Lancashire, Inglaterra, United Kingdom
pt.lifeSummary: Quando Lady Eleanor Holland, Countess March, countess salisbury, baroness chereton nasceu em 13 de outubro de 1370, em Up Holland, Lancashire, Inglaterra, seu pai, Thomas de Holland 2nd Earl of Kent, tinha 20 anos e sua mãe, Alice FitzAlan, tinha 21 anos. Ela casou-se com lord sir Roger Mortimer 4th Earl Of March em 7 de outubro de 1388. Eles tiveram pelo menos 2 filhos e 3 filhas. Ela faleceu em 18 de outubro de 1405, em Benenden, Kent, Inglaterra, United Kingdom, com 35 anos, e foi sepultada em Bisham, Berkshire, Inglaterra, United Kingdom.

ro.Up Holland,Wigan,Lancashire,England: Up Holland, Lancashire, Anglia, United Kingdom

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Lady Eleanor Holland, Countess March, countess salisbury, baroness chereton родилась 13 октября 1370 г. в местности уп холланд, ланкашир, Англия, её отцу по имени Thomas de Holland 2nd Earl of Kent было 20 лет, а матери, которую звали Alice FitzAlan, 21. Она вышла замуж за человека по имени lord sir Roger Mortimer 4th Earl Of March 7 октября 1388 г. У них было по крайней мере 2 сына и 3 дочери. Она умерла 18 октября 1405 г. в местности биненден, кэнт, Англия, Великобритания в возрасте 35 лет и была похоронена в местности бишам, биркшир, Англия, Великобритания.

es.13 October 1370–18 October 1405: 13 de octubre de 1370–18 de octubre de 1405
es.Up Holland,Wigan,Lancashire,England: Up Holland, Lancashire, Inglaterra, Reino Unido
es.lifeSummary: Cuando Lady Eleanor Holland, Countess March, countess salisbury, baroness chereton nació el 13 de octubre de 1370, en Up Holland, Lancashire, Inglaterra, su papá, Thomas de Holland 2nd Earl of Kent, tenía 20 años y su mamá, Alice FitzAlan, tenía 21 años. Se casó con lord sir Roger Mortimer 4th Earl Of March el 7 de octubre de 1388. Fueron padres de por lo menos 2 hijos y 3 hijas. Murió el 18 de octubre de 1405, en Benenden, Kent, Inglaterra, Reino Unido, a la edad de 35 años, y fue sepultada en Bisham, Berkshire, Inglaterra, Reino Unido.
